Assignment 3: Interactive Visualization

Background

For this assignment, you will use the D3 skills you’ve gained over the semester to individually implement an interactive and animated visualization. You will build a visualization that enables interactive exploration or storytelling of a dataset of your own choosing and deploy it on the web.

This assignment has two goals: (1) we want to assess your ability to implement interaction and animation techniques for visualizations individually; and (2) we want you to think carefully about the effectiveness of specific interaction and animation techniques for your chosen data domain. For example, the zipdecode and NameGrapher applications apply the interactive technique of dynamic queries – first explored in the HomeFinder application – to the problem of uncovering patterns in zip codes and baby names. Similarly, interaction and animation techniques have also been very effectively used for explorable explanations, including Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) and machine learning concepts.

A critical challenge will be scoping the assignment such that you can complete it within approximately 2-3 weeks (any more time than this should ideally be spent working on your final projects). Focus on designing a limited yet compelling visualization that enables interactive exploration along a few critical dimensions, and then layer on additional complexity. The NameGrapher application is a nice example that uses a simple but elegant interaction design to enable engaging explorations. A tightly-focused, well-implemented interactive graphic is much preferred to a sprawling design that attempts too much!

Your Tasks

Design an interactive graphic (with any necessary animation techniques) to explore or understand a compelling question for a dataset of your own choosing. If you wish, you may use your dataset from A1, although you may need to add in synthetic data in order to have a dataset large enough to warrant interactive visualization (you can consider using an LLM to help you generate synthetic data).

In order to determine what subset of the data and which interactive options are most promising, we encourage you to perform additional exploratory analysis. What aspects of the data reveal the most interesting discoveries or stories? Do not feel obligated to try to convey everything about the data: focus on a compelling subset.

Your graphic must include interactions and animations that enable exploration or storytelling. Possible techniques include panning, zooming, brushing, details-on-demand (e.g., tooltips), dynamic query filters, and selecting different measures to display. You are free to also consider highlights, annotations, or other narrative features intended to draw attention to particular items of interest and provide additional context.

Implement your graphic in D3.js and deploy it to the web. You may use plug-ins for JS and D3 provided your graphic does not require customized server-side support; you should simply load data from a static data file or public web API.

You should use GitHub pages to host your visualization from your project repository. We recommend keeping everything (development files and website) in your main branch: either serve your website from the root folder or from the /docs folder. Your repo must also contain the (unobfuscated) source code for your visualization.

After you have implemented and deployed your interactive visualization, you will complete a write-up with the following components:

A rationale for your design decisions. How did you choose your particular visual encodings, interaction, and animation techniques? What alternatives did you consider and how did you arrive at your ultimate choices?
An overview of your development process. Describe your working process, including the use of LLMs in helping you write D3 code (if you choose to use them). For the commentary on the development process, consider including answers to the following questions: Roughly how much time did you spend developing your application (in people-hours)? If you used an LLM, did you feel like you were comfortable coding like this? What aspects took the most time?
Remember to acknowledge all appropriate sources not just in your write-up but also directly on your visualization itself (including the source of your data, and any example visualization you drew inspiration from). The write-up should be no longer than two pages long (you are free to use your own spacing and font size conventions, but please be reasonable).

Peer Critique (Thurs, 03/19)

During class time on Thursday 03/19 (3:00pm EST), we will split the class into multiple parallel sessions to demo your A4 work so far and conduct peer design critiques. You MUST have a minimum viable working prototype by this time. The prototype need not be feature-complete or fully polished, but it should demonstrate the core visual encoding, interactive, and/or animated ideas you wish to ultimately include.

During the peer review, you should be ready to answer the following questions (approximately 5 minutes of sharing):

(~1 min) A short introduction to your dataset and goals of your project: what is it that your visualization is trying to help a reader with, and why is it important?
(~1 min) A demonstration of your prototype so far.
(~1-1.5 mins) A discussion of some of the key design decisions you’ve made so far (e.g., did you do any exploratory data analysis and did it reveal something interesting? Why did you pick these particular visual encodings, or interaction and animation techniques? What alternative ideas did you explore or are you considering? etc.).
(~30 secs) A brief list of questions that you would like your peers in the class to comment on as part of their critique. After each presentation, we will have a 1-2 minute Q&A session for your peer reviewers to ask questions.
During this class, each of you will also be responsible for critiquing three projects. We will do this during class time. For each project, we would like you to not only pay close attention to the presentation but also spend some time interacting with the visualization itself. You should then compose a critique following the structure we’ve used in class (“I like… I wish… What if…?”) and cover the following concerns:

Visual Encodings. Are expressive and effective visual encodings applied? How well do they reveal the most important features or trends of the underlying data? Is critical data easily seen, or is it unnecessarily “hidden” and only revealed in response to interaction? Is the target audience likely to understand the visualization?
Interaction & Animation Techniques. Do the supported interaction and animation techniques enable more effective discovery of interesting trends, patterns, or outliers? Do they engage the viewer in a process of meaningful exploration or learning? Are they well-implemented, without notable performance issues or usability problems?
Design Quality. Assess the overall design quality in terms of organization and presentation. Are elements appropriately titled or labeled? Is there appropriate spacing, layout, legible type, and other forms of design styling? Is it clear where to begin viewing/interacting with the design? Is the overall display confusing or cluttered? How successful is the prototype in meeting the intended goals?
Critiques should be detailed enough to be actionable but brief. Don’t forget to share your critiques with your classmates (you might want to share a Google Doc with them). Critiques can be submitted on MarkUs. We will grade your critiques on how thoughtfully you’ve engaged with your classmate’s visualization, and how constructive and actionable your feedback is.

Final Submission (Fri, 03/27)

After the peer review session, you will then have until Friday 03/27, 11:59 pm EST to improve your project based on the feedback of your peers, as well as continue to implement and polish any features that did not make it for the MVP submission. You may use late days to extend this deadline. Make your final submission on Markus. The final submission should consist of a complete working prototype (provided as a URL) and the writeup as detailed above. Your visualization should be accessible on GitHub pages using a URL of the form: https://csc316-student.github.io/cool-interactive-vis/.

Grading

Your grade in this assignment will be broken down into three components:

The Visualization

The visualization is scored out of 15 points. Visualizations that squarely meet the requirements for the assignment will receive a score of 12 out of 15. Going beyond the call of duty can net additional points, for example:

advanced interaction or animation techniques
novel visualization elements
effective multi-view coordination
thoughtful and elegant graphic design
insightful & engaging exploration or narrative experience
Point deductions will be made when projects suffer from:

errors or broken features
clearly ineffective visual encodings
lack of exploratory or narrative interaction or animation techniques
overly simplistic or distracting interaction or animation techniques
confusing interface design
incomplete or insufficient write-up
The In-Class Peer Review Presentation

Presentations will be graded on a 2-point scale as evaluated by one of your TA’s. We will take a look at the progress of your visualization so far and maybe ask 1-2 questions (e.g., did your demo have a narrative arc, did you highlight interesting insights your visualization helps uncover, etc.).

Peer Critique

Each critique you write will be worth a point, for a total of 3 points. We will assess how thoughtfully you’ve engaged with the visualization, and how constructive and actionable your feedback is. Fractions of a point will be deducted for critiques that are shallow or do not offer much constructive feedback.

Submission Details/Checklist

Please make your final submission on Markus. Make sure to include the following:

GitHub pages URL to your final deployed visualization.
Your write-up for the interactive visualization project (max. 2 pages)
You may want to test your visualization on a few devices and browsers (we will use Chrome or Firefox in our evaluations) to ensure there are no technical issues in advance of the final submission deadline.

Based on what's already built, here's a clear picture of where things stand and what comes next.

**What you have (solid MVP):**
- 3D orthographic globe with quaternion-based drag rotation
- Scroll-to-zoom with semantic scaling
- Category filter checkboxes (EMS, Traffic, Fire, Other)
- Hover tooltips (details-on-demand)
- Animated zoom-in on load
- Dark theme aesthetic

**What's missing or broken before peer review (Thu 03/19):**

1. **Subtitle bug** — `index.html` says "10,000 recent emergency calls" but your dataset has ~1,200 points. Easy fix.

2. **No data attribution on the visualization** — The assignment explicitly requires: *"acknowledge all appropriate sources... directly on your visualization itself."* You need a small credit line on the page (e.g., "Data: Montgomery County 911 Calls / Kaggle").

3. **No legend on the visualization** — There are colored checkboxes, but no standalone legend. This is standard for a deployed viz.

4. **GitHub Pages deployment needs verification** — The expected URL format is `https://csc316-student.github.io/<repo-name>/`. You need to confirm GitHub Pages is enabled in your repo settings and the `index.html` loads correctly with the relative paths (`911_sampled.csv`, `countries-110m.json`).

**What would elevate your grade beyond 12/15:**

- **Time-based slider** — Filter calls by hour-of-day or day-of-week. Your data has `timeStamp` already.
- **Small coordinated bar/donut chart** — Show category breakdown counts that update as you filter. This counts as "multi-view coordination."
- **Fly-to animation** — When you first load, animate the globe rotating from a global view to zoom in on Pennsylvania.
- **Cluster/density view** — At low zoom levels show density hexbins; at high zoom show individual points.

**For final submission (Fri 03/27) you still need:**

- The 2-page **write-up** covering design rationale, development process, and LLM usage.
- Final GitHub Pages URL submitted on Markus.

**Immediate priority order:**

1. Fix the subtitle count bug
2. Add data attribution footer to `index.html`
3. Verify GitHub Pages is live and working
4. Add a time-of-day filter or coordinated chart (before 03/19 peer review if possible, otherwise after)
5. Write the writeup after peer feedback

Want me to start with fixing the bugs and adding the attribution/legend directly to your files?

_**Agent (gpt-5.3-codex)**_

Upgraded the project into a much more advanced, story-driven interactive visualization with coordinated views and animation.

### What I changed

- **Fixed a critical JS load bug** in `main.js` (`themes` was used before declaration).
- Added a **story panel overlay** in `index.html` with:
  - Play / Pause controls
  - Hour slider (`0–23`) for temporal scrubbing
  - Narrative chapter buttons: **Morning Rush**, **Afternoon Peak**, **Night Shift**
  - Live KPI cards: visible calls, top category, top township
  - Embedded mini chart area
- Implemented **hour-by-hour storytelling logic** in `main.js`:
  - Calls now animate through time-of-day
  - Slider + autoplay drive the scene
  - Category filters and time are combined (coordinated filtering)
- Added a **coordinated mini bar chart** (D3) that updates by current hour + selected categories.
- Added **animated hotspot callouts** (top 3 townships for current time slice) with pulsing rings.
- Added dynamic narrative text that changes as the hour changes (commute, midday, evening, overnight).
- Kept and integrated your **day/night theme toggle** with the new story UI.
- Improved responsiveness and styling in `style.css` for panel, controls, KPI cards, and mobile-ish widths.

### Files updated

- `index.html`
- `main.js`
- `style.css`

### Why this is stronger for CSC316

This now demonstrates multiple advanced D3 skills at once:

- Temporal storytelling + animation
- Dynamic queries (category + hour)
- Coordinated multi-view design (map + KPI + bar chart)
- Details-on-demand + animated annotation layer (hotspots)
- Polished visual design and interaction flow

If you want, next I can add one more “wow” feature: **guided camera fly-to transitions per chapter** (auto-rotate globe to different hotspot regions with smooth easing) so your in-class demo has a stronger narrative arc.

  - Loads region boundaries for:

    - `Total Calls`
    - `EMS Share`
    - `Traffic Share`

    - `Dominant Category`

  - Seasonal behavior + country differences
  - Supports **Regenerate Data** button for fresh scenarios each run

- Updated storytelling controls in `index.html`:
  - Month slider + play/pause animation

  - KPI cards now show:
    - Calls this month
    - Top region

  - Risk index

- Styling updates in `style.css`:

  - Keep your synthetic aggregate storytelling layer and hover “Top Story Report”

te\\\":{\\\"rootPromptMessagesJson\\\":[],\\\"turnsOld\\\":[],\\\"todos\\\":[],\\\"pendingToolCalls\\\":[],\\\"turns\\\":[],\\\"previousWorkspaceUris\\\":[],\\\"fileStates\\\":{},\\\"summaryArchives\\\":[],\\\"turnTimings\\\":[],\\\"fileStatesV2\\\":{},\\\"subagentStates\\\":{},\\\"selfSummaryCount\\\":0,\\\"readPaths\\\":[]},\\\"text\\\":\\\"\\\",\\\"toolFormerData\\\":{\\\"toolCallId\\\":\\\"toolu_bdrk_01T8UzVW2bBGfrxKkooYpLSq\\\",\\\"toolIndex\\\":0,\\\"modelCallId\\\":\\\"toolu_bdrk_01T8UzVW2bBGfrxKkooYpLSq\\\",\\\"status\\\":\\\"completed\\\",\\\"name\\\":\\\"read_file_v2\\\",\\\"rawArgs\\\":\\\"{\\\\\\\"path\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"/Users/sahilhanda/.cursor/projects/Users-sahilhanda-Desktop-projects-csc316-a3-csc316-A3/mcps/cursor-ide-browser/INSTRUCTIONS.md\\\\\\\"}\\\",\\\"tool\\\":40,\\\"params\\\":\\\"{\\\\\\\"targetFile\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"/Users/sahilhanda/.cursor/projects/Users-sahilhanda-Desktop-projects-csc316-a3-csc316-A3/mcps/cursor-ide-browser/INSTRUCTIONS.md\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"charsLimit\\\\\\\":1000000,\\\\\\\"effectiveUri\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"/Users/sahilhanda/.cursor/projects/Users-sahilhanda-Desktop-projects-csc316-a3-csc316-A3/mcps/cursor-ide-browser/INSTRUCTIONS.md\\\\\\\"}\\\",\\\"result\\\":\\\"{\\\\\\\"contents\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"The cursor-ide-browser is an MCP server that allows you to navigate the web and interact with the page. Use this for frontend/webapp development and testing code changes.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nCRITICAL - Lock/unlock workflow:\\\\\\\\n1. browser_lock requires an existing browser tab - you CANNOT lock before browser_navigate\\\\\\\\n2. Correct order: browser_navigate -\u003e browser_lock -\u003e (interactions) -\u003e browser_unlock\\\\\\\\n3. If a browser tab already exists (check with browser_tabs list), call browser_lock FIRST before any interactions\\\\\\\\n4. Only call browser_unlock when completely done with ALL browser operations for this turn\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nIMPORTANT - Before interacting with any page:\\\\\\\\n1. Use browser_tabs with action \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"list\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" to see open tabs and their URLs\\\\\\\\n2. Use browser_snapshot to get the page structure and element refs before any interaction (click, type, hover, etc.)\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nIMPORTANT - Waiting strategy:\\\\\\\\nWhen waiting for page changes (navigation, content loading, animations, etc.), prefer short incremental waits (1-3 seconds) with browser_snapshot checks in between rather than a single long wait. For example, instead of waiting 10 seconds, do: wait 2s -\u003e snapshot -\u003e check if ready -\u003e if not, wait 2s more -\u003e snapshot again. This allows you to proceed as soon as the page is ready rather than always waiting the maximum time.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nPERFORMANCE PROFILING:\\\\\\\\n- browser_profile_start/stop: CPU profiling with call stacks and timing data. Use to identify slow JavaScript functions.\\\\\\\\n- Profile data is written to ~/.cursor/browser-logs/. Files: cpu-profile-{timestamp}.json (raw profile in Chrome DevTools format) and cpu-profile-{timestamp}-summary.md (human-readable summary).\\\\\\\\n- IMPORTANT: When investigating performance issues, read the raw cpu-profile-*.json file to verify summary data. Key fields: profile.samples.length (total samples), profile.nodes[].hitCount (per-node hits), profile.nodes[].callFrame.functionName (function names). Cross-reference with the summary to confirm findings before making optimization recommendations.\\\\\\\\n\\\\\\\\nNotes:\\\\\\\\n- Native dialogs (alert/confirm/prompt) never block automation. By default, confirm() returns true and prompt() returns the default value. To test different responses, call browser_handle_dialog BEFORE the triggering action: use accept: false for \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"Cancel\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\", or promptText: \\\\\\\\\\\\\\\"value\\\\\\\\\\\\\\\" for custom prompt input.\\\\\\\\n- Iframe content is not accessible - only elements outside iframes can be interacted with.\\\\\\\\n- Use browser_type to append text, browser_fill to clear and replace. browser_fill also works on contenteditable elements.\\\\\\\\n- For nested scroll containers, use browser_scroll with scrollIntoView: true before clicking elements that may be obscured.\\\\\\\",\\\\\\\"totalLinesInFile\\\\\\\":25}\\\",\\\"toolCallBinary\\\":\\\"QgA=\\\",\\\"toolCall\\\":\\\"{\\\\\\\"readToolCall\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"args\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"path\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"/Users/sahilhanda/.cursor/projects/Users-sahilhanda-Desktop-projects-csc316-a3-csc316-A3/mcps/cursor-ide-browser/INSTRUCTIONS.md\\\\\\\"},\\\\\\\"result\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"success\\\\\\\":{\\\\\\\"content\\\\\\\":\\\\\\\"The cursor-ide-browser is an MCP server that allows you to navigate the web and interact with the page.     c=g['coordinates']

        for ring in c:
            for x,y in ring:
                yield x,y
    elif t=='MultiPolygon':

```bash
python3 - <<'PY'
import json, pathlib
base=pathlib.Path('/Users/sahilhanda/Desktop/projects/csc316/a3/csc316-A3/data')
all_feats=[]
for fn in ['us-states.geojson','canada-provinces.geojson','mexico-states.geojson']:
    d=json.loads((base/fn).read_text())
    all_feats.extend(d['features'])

def iter_coords(g):
    t=g['type']
    c=g['coordinates']
    if t=='Polygon':
        for ring in c:
            for x,y in ring:
                yield x,y
    elif t=='MultiPolygon':
        for poly in c:
            for ring in poly:
                for x,y in ring:
                    yield x,y

mins=[1e9,1e9]; maxs=[-1e9,-1e9]
for f in all_feats:
    for x,y in iter_coords(f['geometry']):
        mins[0]=min(mins[0],x); mins[1]=min(mins[1],y)
        maxs[0]=max(maxs[0],x); maxs[1]=max(maxs[1],y)
print('bbox',mins,maxs)
PY
```

_**Agent (gpt-5.3-codex)**_

You’re absolutely right, and the screenshot helped pinpoint it.

I fixed the map to match the style direction you want:

- **Clear state/province boundaries** with stronger linework
- **One solid color per region** (dominant emergency type)
- **No fine dots / no point marks**
- Hover still shows:
  - aggregate emergency stats
  - the “Top Story Report” narrative details

### What was causing the broken red block look
- Some source boundary coordinates were outside normal longitude range, which created a wrapped polygon artifact and bad fill behavior.
- The synthetic category model was over-biasing one category, so colors lacked variety.

### What I changed
- Sanitized region geometry longitudes before render (prevents wrap artifacts).
- Reworked synthetic aggregation profiles to produce realistic **category diversity across regions**.
- Strengthened `.region` border styling for map-like separation.
- Kept region-level choropleth only (no bubbles/dots).
- Aligned legend, bar chart, tooltip tags, and map colors so everything uses the same palette.

### One thing to do now
Please hard refresh once more (`Cmd+Shift+R`) so you see the newest render logic and styling together.

If you want, next I can add a subtle **region hover highlight + selected-state lock on click** so demos are easier when presenting.

Implemented a full fix for both issues:

- **Globe rendering is restored** (proper orthographic globe with ocean + land + graticule)
- **State/province colors are stable and meaningful**

### What I changed

  - Hides regions on the back hemisphere correctly
  - Supports drag-to-rotate and scroll-to-zoom

- Fixed geometry normalization:

- Improved temporal aggregation:
  - Added monthly category multipliers so dominant category genuinely shifts by month.
  - During **Play Story**, `activeMonth` advances and region fills update accordingly.

- Kept your required interaction model:
  - One consistent color per state/province (dominant category for current month)
  - Hover tooltip with aggregate metrics + “Top Story Report”

- Minor UI alignment:
  - Updated instructions text to “Drag to rotate”.

### Files updated

- `main.js` (major rewrite)
- `style.css` (globe + region styling refinements)
- `index.html` (instruction text)

You should now see a proper globe with region-based coloring that evolves as story playback advances through time.

_**Agent (gpt-5.3-codex)**_

Fixed — that Virginia “stretch across the globe” bug should now be resolved.

### What I changed
- In `main.js`, I changed longitude sanitization from **wrapping** to **clamping**:
  - Old behavior could map values like `-188` to `+172`, which creates giant cross-globe polygons.
  - New behavior clamps to the dateline (`-179.999` to `179.999`) so polygons stay local.

### Why this matters
- Orthographic globe + wrapped longitudes can produce huge region paths that capture hover everywhere.
- Clamping prevents those malformed spans and keeps each state/province shape bounded.

### What to do now
- Refresh the page (hard refresh recommended once).
- Test hover on Virginia again and nearby regions.
- `Play Story` will still animate month-to-month dominant-category color changes as requested.

If any one specific region still behaves oddly, I can add a second safety pass that rewinds ring orientation for problematic polygons.

&#96;d3.geoMercator()&#96; projection (line 186)\\\\n   - Mercator cannot properly handle geometries that cross the antimeridian (±180° longitude)\\\\n   - This causes Alaska to render as a massive stretched polygon that likely covers most/all of the map\\\\n\\\\n3. **Code still references old TopoJSON**\\\\n   - Line 439 of &#96;main.js&#96; contains: _**Agent (gpt-5.3-codex)**_

You were right to push on this. I checked via agent-driven investigation and applied a targeted fix for the “Virginia spans the globe” behavior.

### What was actually happening
A few complex multipolygon geometries can render as an **inverse fill** on a globe (especially near antimeridian/polar boundaries).  
When that happens, one region behaves like it covers almost everything, so hover and color look broken.

### Fixes I applied
- Added `fill-rule: evenodd` to `.region` in `style.css` to prevent inverse-fill artifacts.
- Added geometry repair in `main.js`:
  - sanitize longitudes
  - detect suspiciously inverted spherical polygons (`d3.geoArea(feature) > 2π`)
  - flip ring order to correct fill/hit area
- Bumped cache-busting version so browser definitely loads latest code:
  - `style.css?v=regional-v4`
  - `main.js?v=regional-v4`

### What to do now
Refresh localhost once (normal refresh should be enough now due version bump).  
Then test by hovering Virginia and nearby states — hover area should be local, not globe-wide, and play-story color transitions should still work month-by-month.

If one specific region still misbehaves, I can add a final strict guard to exclude only that malformed ring while keeping the rest of the state/province geometry.

review the assingment handout to ensure we are not missing anything and what the next steps are: CSC316

Assignment 3: Interactive Visualization

Background

For this assignment, you will use the D3 skills you’ve gained over the semester to individually implement an interactive and animated visualization. You will build a visualization that enables interactive exploration or storytelling of a dataset of your own choosing and deploy it on the web.

This assignment has two goals: (1) we want to assess your ability to implement interaction and animation techniques for visualizations individually; and (2) we want you to think carefully about the effectiveness of specific interaction and animation techniques for your chosen data domain. For example, the zipdecode and NameGrapher applications apply the interactive technique of dynamic queries – first explored in the HomeFinder application – to the problem of uncovering patterns in zip codes and baby names. Similarly, interaction and animation techniques have also been very effectively used for explorable explanations, including Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) and machine learning concepts.

A critical challenge will be scoping the assignment such that you can complete it within approximately 2-3 weeks (any more time than this should ideally be spent working on your final projects). Focus on designing a limited yet compelling visualization that enables interactive exploration along a few critical dimensions, and then layer on additional complexity. The NameGrapher application is a nice example that uses a simple but elegant interaction design to enable engaging explorations. A tightly-focused, well-implemented interactive graphic is much preferred to a sprawling design that attempts too much!

Your Tasks

Design an interactive graphic (with any necessary animation techniques) to explore or understand a compelling question for a dataset of your own choosing. If you wish, you may use your dataset from A1, although you may need to add in synthetic data in order to have a dataset large enough to warrant interactive visualization (you can consider using an LLM to help you generate synthetic data).

In order to determine what subset of the data and which interactive options are most promising, we encourage you to perform additional exploratory analysis. What aspects of the data reveal the most interesting discoveries or stories? Do not feel obligated to try to convey everything about the data: focus on a compelling subset.

Your graphic must include interactions and animations that enable exploration or storytelling. Possible techniques include panning, zooming, brushing, details-on-demand (e.g., tooltips), dynamic query filters, and selecting different measures to display. You are free to also consider highlights, annotations, or other narrative features intended to draw attention to particular items of interest and provide additional context.

Implement your graphic in D3.js and deploy it to the web. You may use plug-ins for JS and D3 provided your graphic does not require customized server-side support; you should simply load data from a static data file or public web API.

You should use GitHub pages to host your visualization from your project repository. We recommend keeping everything (development files and website) in your main branch: either serve your website from the root folder or from the /docs folder. Your repo must also contain the (unobfuscated) source code for your visualization.

After you have implemented and deployed your interactive visualization, you will complete a write-up with the following components:

A rationale for your design decisions. How did you choose your particular visual encodings, interaction, and animation techniques? What alternatives did you consider and how did you arrive at your ultimate choices?
An overview of your development process. Describe your working process, including the use of LLMs in helping you write D3 code (if you choose to use them). For the commentary on the development process, consider including answers to the following questions: Roughly how much time did you spend developing your application (in people-hours)? If you used an LLM, did you feel like you were comfortable coding like this? What aspects took the most time?
Remember to acknowledge all appropriate sources not just in your write-up but also directly on your visualization itself (including the source of your data, and any example visualization you drew inspiration from). The write-up should be no longer than two pages long (you are free to use your own spacing and font size conventions, but please be reasonable).

Peer Critique (Thurs, 03/19)

During class time on Thursday 03/19 (3:00pm EST), we will split the class into multiple parallel sessions to demo your A4 work so far and conduct peer design critiques. You MUST have a minimum viable working prototype by this time. The prototype need not be feature-complete or fully polished, but it should demonstrate the core visual encoding, interactive, and/or animated ideas you wish to ultimately include.

During the peer review, you should be ready to answer the following questions (approximately 5 minutes of sharing):

(~1 min) A short introduction to your dataset and goals of your project: what is it that your visualization is trying to help a reader with, and why is it important?
(~1 min) A demonstration of your prototype so far.
(~1-1.5 mins) A discussion of some of the key design decisions you’ve made so far (e.g., did you do any exploratory data analysis and did it reveal something interesting? Why did you pick these particular visual encodings, or interaction and animation techniques? What alternative ideas did you explore or are you considering? etc.).
(~30 secs) A brief list of questions that you would like your peers in the class to comment on as part of their critique. After each presentation, we will have a 1-2 minute Q&A session for your peer reviewers to ask questions.
During this class, each of you will also be responsible for critiquing three projects. We will do this during class time. For each project, we would like you to not only pay close attention to the presentation but also spend some time interacting with the visualization itself. You should then compose a critique following the structure we’ve used in class (“I like… I wish… What if…?”) and cover the following concerns:

Visual Encodings. Are expressive and effective visual encodings applied? How well do they reveal the most important features or trends of the underlying data? Is critical data easily seen, or is it unnecessarily “hidden” and only revealed in response to interaction? Is the target audience likely to understand the visualization?
Interaction & Animation Techniques. Do the supported interaction and animation techniques enable more effective discovery of interesting trends, patterns, or outliers? Do they engage the viewer in a process of meaningful exploration or learning? Are they well-implemented, without notable performance issues or usability problems?
Design Quality. Assess the overall design quality in terms of organization and presentation. Are elements appropriately titled or labeled? Is there appropriate spacing, layout, legible type, and other forms of design styling? Is it clear where to begin viewing/interacting with the design? Is the overall display confusing or cluttered? How successful is the prototype in meeting the intended goals?
Critiques should be detailed enough to be actionable but brief. Don’t forget to share your critiques with your classmates (you might want to share a Google Doc with them). Critiques can be submitted on MarkUs. We will grade your critiques on how thoughtfully you’ve engaged with your classmate’s visualization, and how constructive and actionable your feedback is.

Final Submission (Fri, 03/27)

After the peer review session, you will then have until Friday 03/27, 11:59 pm EST to improve your project based on the feedback of your peers, as well as continue to implement and polish any features that did not make it for the MVP submission. You may use late days to extend this deadline. Make your final submission on Markus. The final submission should consist of a complete working prototype (provided as a URL) and the writeup as detailed above. Your visualization should be accessible on GitHub pages using a URL of the form: https://csc316-student.github.io/cool-interactive-vis/.

Grading

Your grade in this assignment will be broken down into three components:

The Visualization

The visualization is scored out of 15 points. Visualizations that squarely meet the requirements for the assignment will receive a score of 12 out of 15. Going beyond the call of duty can net additional points, for example:

advanced interaction or animation techniques
novel visualization elements
effective multi-view coordination
thoughtful and elegant graphic design
insightful & engaging exploration or narrative experience
Point deductions will be made when projects suffer from:

errors or broken features
clearly ineffective visual encodings
lack of exploratory or narrative interaction or animation techniques
overly simplistic or distracting interaction or animation techniques
confusing interface design
incomplete or insufficient write-up
The In-Class Peer Review Presentation

Presentations will be graded on a 2-point scale as evaluated by one of your TA’s. We will take a look at the progress of your visualization so far and maybe ask 1-2 questions (e.g., did your demo have a narrative arc, did you highlight interesting insights your visualization helps uncover, etc.).

Peer Critique

Each critique you write will be worth a point, for a total of 3 points. We will assess how thoughtfully you’ve engaged with the visualization, and how constructive and actionable your feedback is. Fractions of a point will be deducted for critiques that are shallow or do not offer much constructive feedback.

Submission Details/Checklist

Please make your final submission on Markus. Make sure to include the following:

GitHub pages URL to your final deployed visualization.
Your write-up for the interactive visualization project (max. 2 pages)
You may want to test your visualization on a few devices and browsers (we will use Chrome or Firefox in our evaluations) to ensure there are no technical issues in advance of the final submission deadline.
